channel.title: RSS Aggregator Feed
channel.description: An aggregated feed of Technology-related news

item.title: Your AI agents need a terminal, not just a vector database
item.description: When agentic workflows fail, developers often assume the problem lies in the underlying model’s reasoning abilities. In reality, the limited information provided by the retrieval interface is often the primary limiting factor.Researchers at multiple universities propose a technique called direct corpus interaction (DCI) that lets agents bypass embedding models entirely, searching raw corpora directly using standard command-line tools.The limits of classic retrievalIn classic retrieval systems such as RAG, documents are chunked, converted into vector representations (or embeddings), and indexed...

item.title: Google goes for the glitter with disco-ball icons: ‘Are y’all sure you still want this?’
item.description: You can now disco ball-ify your entire Pixel homescreen, says Google.

item.title: Corsair DDR5 stick spotted using Chinese memory chips — here's why that could mean the RAM crisis ends sooner than expected
item.description: Is this a sign that the predicted memory cavalry is rushing to the rescue?

item.title: How VCs and founders use inflated ‘ARR’ to crown AI startups
item.description: Some AI startups are stretching traditional revenue metrics when talking about progress publicly. And their investors are fully aware.

item.title: Google’s AI search is so broken it can ‘disregard’ what you’re looking for
item.description: Google's AI Overviews are running into an interesting problem right now. Earlier on Friday, if you searched for the term "disregard," the AI Overview section would include a response like what you'd see from a more traditional AI chatbot instead of the typical AI summary, as spotted on X. As you can see in the […]

item.title: Google is currently struggling to define words like disregard, stop and ignore
item.description: The search engine's definitions have been replaced with AI Overviews.

item.title: How the costume designer of ‘I Love Boosters’ brought color back to Hollywood
item.description: Oscar-nominated costume designer Shirley Kurata breaks down her technicolor work on the new mind-bending satire of the fashion industry. In the world of I Love Boosters, color reigns supreme.

item.title: In a sea of identical smartphones, Nothing stands out
item.description: By building for culture, community, and “vibes first,” the brand is proving that being different still sells in consumer tech. I was in a pub in London, catching up with my oldest friends. One friend turned to another: “What is that phone you are holding?”

item.title: 'We know that fans will love them' — Assassin's Creed Black Flag Resynced creative director says Ubisoft changed the music's execution in the remake and added new scores for some 'emotional moments' of the game
item.description: The music in Assassin's Creed Black Flag Resynced has been tweaked, and new scores have been added to complement some of the more significant story quests.

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s decade-old lesson on navigating uncertainty is more relevant than ever
item.description: As the late-night host signs off from CBS, his 2015 Wake Forest University commencement speech feels especially timely for the class of 2026. 2015 was a different time. One of the most polarizing discussions online was about whether the color of a striped dress was blue and black or white and gold. The class of 2015 was graduating into an election year. And Stephen Colbert was just gearing up to become the host of “The Late Show.”...

item.title: The care economy is aging
item.description: Why menopause investment can’t wait. At 3 a.m., in the midst of labor, a doula we’ll call Renee stepped into the hallway to steady herself.

item.title: ‘Hire a damn artist’: Los Angeles magazine gets swift backlash for AI cover that aimed to be subversive
item.description: The publication wanted to start a conversation. It may not be getting the one it signed up for. AI-generated images are already all over the internet, and now magazines are getting in on the act, too.

item.title: Cisco tried using AI to write security incident reports — and things didn't really go as planned
item.description: There are many challenges with generating long-form reports with AI, even with specific sources and instructions.
